_meta.description: Portfolio summary framework — defining insight, 3-cluster structure, 4-question screening, and catalyst table for all 13 verticals.

defining_insight.title: The defining structural insight: three verticals simultaneously at inning 1-2 with the highest capex gaps in the universe
defining_insight.body: Nuclear/Uranium (inning 2, capex gap 90), REE (inning 1, capex gap 92), and Electricity/Grid (inning 2, capex gap 88) all share the same structural profile: demand is mathematically locked in (AI power + enrichment rebuild + grid buildout), supply chains are under-invested after a decade+ of neglect, and lead times are so long that even committed investment today cannot solve the deficit quickly. Business cycle risk scores for all three are below 30 — meaning they are the most defensible positions in a recessionary environment. This combination of locked demand + scarce supply + long lead times + recession-resistance is the definition of an exceptional structural investment thesis.

clusters.title: Early innings (1-3) — highest conviction structural longs
items: REE (inning 1): MP Materials (MP), Lynas (LYC.AX) — entire Western supply chain barely exists
Nuclear/Uranium (inning 2): Centrus LEU, Cameco CCO, SPUT — enrichment rebuild year 1-2
Electricity/Grid (inning 2): Quanta PWR, Eaton ETN, GE Vernova GEV — transformer bottleneck 2-4yr
Copper (inning 2): SCCO, FCX, FNV/WPM streams — 16-17yr mine development lag
Tech Metals (inning 2): Perpetua PPTA, Alphamin AFMJF — export controls already active
Water/Land (inning 2): ERII, VWTR — scarcity recognition just beginning
NG/LNG (inning 3): LNG, VGAS, KMI, ET — European SPAs locked 20yr

clusters.title: Mid innings (4-6) — structural but needs patience or entry timing
items: Li/Battery (inning 4): ALB, SQM, FLNC — cyclical trough, 2026-2030 demand wave
Fertilizers (inning 4): NTR, MOS, ERII — post-2022 spike normalization
Ind. Inputs (inning 3): LIN, APD, ATI — strategic but mid-cycle in most sub-chains
PGMs/Gold/Ag (inning 4): FNV, WPM, gold streamers — Pt hydrogen wave long-dated
Oil (inning 5): VLO, TDW, STNG — mid-cycle selective, not primary structural thesis

clusters.title: Business cycle sensitivity — how to differentiate within early innings
items: Most recession-resistant: Nuclear (bc:20), REE (bc:28), Grid/Electricity (bc:22), Water (bc:32) — policy-driven, not economically discretionary
Moderate cycle sensitivity: NG/LNG (bc:52), Ind.Inputs (bc:38), Fertilizers (bc:52) — some industrial demand cyclicality
Most cycle-sensitive: Oil (bc:72), Li/Battery (bc:68), Copper (bc:62) — China construction, EV consumer credit, commodity price beta
Key implication: In a recession scenario, overweight Nuclear + REE + Grid + Water and underweight Copper and Lithium until the structural deficit signal reasserts.

four_questions.a: The most important question. Inning 1-2 = maximum future return potential but requires patience. Inning 7-9 = most of the structural thesis is priced in. In this universe, the average position is inning 2.9 — early across the board.

four_questions.q: 2. What is the capex gap score?
four_questions.a: A high capex gap (>80) means the sector has been starved of investment for a decade+. Under-investment creates future price support because supply cannot quickly respond to demand signals. Nuclear (90) and REE (92) are the extreme cases.

four_questions.q: 3. How sensitive is it to the business cycle?
four_questions.a: A position with inning 1 + bc risk >65 (like Copper or Lithium) requires more timing care than one with inning 1 + bc risk <30 (like Nuclear or REE). In a recessionary environment, the low-bc-risk early-innings positions are most defensible.

Even in the most structurally compelling vertical, the structure of the position matters enormously. Streaming (FNV, WPM) is better than mining in many verticals. Physical trusts (SPUT) are better than equity for uranium in early innings. Royalties sit above the processing chokepoint — geopolitically optimal.
